title: Chapter Two: Champagne and Silk
content: The service entrance smells like bleach and burnt butter.

I push through the swinging door into a kitchen that's already at war — caterers shouting across the line, a sous chef slamming a pan onto the range hard enough to rattle the hanging copper, and somewhere behind the steam table, someone is crying. I don't look. Crying in a kitchen is like crying in a hospital: everyone pretends they can't hear it because acknowledging it would mean stopping, and no one here can afford to stop.

A woman in a black blazer shoves a uniform at me — cheap poly-blend, the kind that pills after two washes, the buttonholes already fraying. I hold it up. The shoulders are wrong by two inches. The waist hits me at the ribs. The seams are topstitched where they should be fell-stitched, and the fabric doesn't drape — it *hangs*, like a sentence that forgot its verb.
"Tray's at station three," the woman says without looking at me. "Flutes only. Don't talk to the guests."

I step into the staff bathroom to change. The uniform is worse on than off — tight across my back, loose at the hips, the collar scratching my throat like it resents me. I run my fingers along the side seam as I walk back out, pinching and easing the thread between my thumbnail and forefinger. Pure instinct. My fingers know what my brain shouldn't bother with: this fabric won't hold a re-seam, but the tension release at the waist could be fixed with four minutes and a running stitch. I could make it fit. I could make it *movable*. I adjust the seams as I walk — micro-shifts, the shoulder forward, the side panel released — and by the time I reach station three, the uniform sits better. Not good. Better.

The champagne flutes are arranged in rows on my tray, sixty-four dollars a bottle, the bubbles rising in neat little columns. I taste one at the corner of my lip when I lift the tray — dry, citric, the effervescence pinching at the soft tissue like tiny teeth. Good champagne. I'll give them that.
The ballroom doors open.
It opens before me like a wound.

Crystal chandeliers scatter light across three hundred people, refracting into the silk and satin and organza below until the whole room gleams like the inside of a geode. Candles — real ones, beeswax, I can smell the honey-fat beneath the cloud of perfume that hits me like a wall — line the perimeter on iron stands. The air is thick with it: jasmine, oud, the particular vanilla-arsenic of Tom Ford Noir, and beneath all of it, the green-stem scent of lilies arranged in displays that cost more than my rent.
I step into the room and start pouring.

The first gown catches me at the edge of the crowd — a Dior, fall collection, the pleats supposed to fall in a precise heliocentric spiral from the left hip. They don't. They're misaligned by three millimeters at the waist, which doesn't sound like much unless you understand that three millimeters at the waist becomes three centimeters at the knee, and by the time the fabric reaches the floor, the woman wearing it looks slightly tilted, like a painting hung by someone who didn't use a level. The Dior is beautiful at a distance. Up close, it's a lie told with silk.
I move deeper.

A Versace in chartreuse — and my teeth set on edge immediately, because chartreuse is a color that should come with a warning label and a cardiologist. The woman wearing it has a warm olive undertone that the acid-green washes out completely, pulling every drop of vitality from her face until she looks like she's recovering from something. She spent — what? Twelve thousand? Fifteen? — to look ill. A simple shift to deep emerald would have made her glow. But no one asked me. No one ever asks the help.

The silk of another guest's skirt brushes my arm as she passes — the whisper of it, the weight, pure mulberry, probably four hundred thread count or better, and for one second my fingers *itch* with the want of that fabric between my hands. Then she's gone, trailing her beautiful expensive hem across the marble floor like it's nothing, like silk is just something you walk in.

A McQueen knockoff crosses my path and I have to look away. The seam line on the corsetry is *crooked*. McQueen never left a crooked line in his life, and this approximation — this hollow echo of his silhouette — makes my teeth hurt the way a wrong note does. The bone structure is there, the shadow of genius, but it's been reproduced by someone who understood the shape without understanding the *blood* that made it. A photograph of a photograph. It's the saddest thing I've seen all night, and I've seen a woman cry over crème brûlée.

I place each observation like a scalpel cut. Clean. Precise. Bloodless. This is my territory — fabric, drape, thread, silhouette — and I know it the way a cartographer knows his coastline, the way a surgeon knows the map of veins beneath skin. I just can't be seen in it. Tonight I am a woman in a poly-blend uniform carrying sixty-four-dollar champagne to people who think fabric is what you wear, not what you *become*.
Then I see her.

She's in white — all white, head to toe, a canvas of it. The dress is structured, architectural, not a single ornament, and for a terrible half-second I can't find a single flaw in it. The construction is immaculate. The silhouette is spare and lethal, the kind of minimalism that costs more than excess because it permits no distractions. She might be the only person in this room whose clothes are telling the truth.
Maren Voss.

I don't know how I know her name except that the city has a way of putting names on people like labels on wine. She's standing near the east alcove, one hand resting on the back of a chair she has no intention of sitting in, watching the room like a predator at a zoo. Not hungry. Not threatened. Just *cataloguing*. Her eyes move from face to face the way mine move from seam to seam — with a quiet, clinical appreciation for what's been done well and what's been done badly.
Her gaze reaches me.

One second. Maybe less. Her eyes pass over my face, my uniform, my tray, and then through me — *through* me — as if I were glass. As if I were the space between things rather than a thing myself. There's no malice in it. No dismissal. Dismissal would require acknowledgment. What Maren Voss gives me is less than that. I am furniture. I am the decorative negative space that makes the room's positive space visible.
I hold my tray tighter and keep moving.

The two men are standing near the bar, which means they're standing near me, because the bar is where the trays go to die. One is old money, the kind that doesn't announce itself — his suit is Savile Row, perfectly fitted, wholly unremarkable, and that's the point. The other is newer to this, louder, a watch too thick on his wrist, his cufflinks competing for attention.

"Sotheby's authenticated it themselves," the loud one is saying. "The utility coat. 1940s. French Resistance. They've got documentation — the original owner's name, the sewing kit she carried in the lining. Incredible provenance. It's the centerpiece of the live auction tonight."
My hand stops halfway to the tray.
"Sixty thousand, they're estimating," the quiet one says. "Worth every cent. You're not just buying a coat. You're buying a story."

The champagne flute I'm holding trembles. A bead of condensation slides down the crystal and falls to my wrist, cold and sudden, and I feel it like a shock. The tray dips — I catch it, barely, the flutes chiming against each other in a tiny crystal scream — and I set the whole thing down on the bar with a careful, deliberate motion that costs me every ounce of restraint I have.
French. They said *French*.

I wore that coat. I wore it in London, in 1943, through a winter so cold the Thames wore a skin of ice and the bombs fell every night like a devotional practice the city couldn't stop performing. I wore it when the shelters were full and the ground shook and someone — a woman whose name I have deliberately forgotten, whose face I have not — pressed a cup of tar-black coffee into my hands and said, *You'll live through this, you stubborn creature.* I spilled that coffee. The stain is on the left cuff. Sotheby's missed it, or they didn't care, or they looked at it and thought: *coffee, wartime, authentic.* They didn't know it was *my* coffee. They didn't know the stain is shaped like a small and angry continent.

I press my back against the wall behind the bar. The plaster is cool through the cheap uniform. I breathe in — beeswax, oud, the yeast-sharp note of champagne — and I breathe out, and I do it again, and my heart slams against my ribs like something trying to escape.

The stitch count on the lining is two hundred and fourteen per panel. I know this because I reinforced those stitches myself, sitting in a basement in Whitechapel while the ground shook above me, using a needle I'd stolen from a dead woman's sewing kit and thread I'd unwound from the hem of a curtain. The name sewn into the collar was mine — is mine — stitched in a shade of navy that was already fading the night I sewed it, and I know, I *know*, that the thread gave up its color completely in 1953, because I was there when it did, standing in a laundry in Kensington watching the last of the blue bleed into a sink full of gray water like a small surrender.

They're going to sell it. To someone who will hang it in a climate-controlled display case, or drape it over a mannequin, or — worse — wear it to a party like this one, where people will say *how authentic* and *what a story* and not one of them will know what it felt like to pull that coat tight while the sky came apart.
"Hey."

Suki materializes beside me, tray balanced on one hand, gum snapping between her teeth. She's better at this than I am — the service, the invisible act. She wears invisibility like it was tailored for her. For me it's a loan that doesn't fit.
"You look like you've seen a ghost," she says.
"Something like that."

Suki shifts her tray to her other hand. Her eyes flick across my face, reading whatever's there, and I watch her decide — in real time — how much to push. She's learned. It took her three jobs and one very long conversation at two a.m. on a fire escape in Bed-Stuy, but she's learned.
"The coat in the auction," she says, casual, like she's commenting on the weather. "They're saying it's from the French Resistance."
My voice goes flat. I can't help it. The words come out level and hard, like stones dropped onto a frozen pond: "It's not French."

Suki's gum stops mid-chew. She looks at me — really looks, not the passing glance she gives everything else, but the deep look, the one where she's deciding whether I'm someone she should be worried about or someone she should be worried *for*.
"How would you —"
She stops. Her mouth closes. The gum starts again, slower, and she turns back to the room, adjusting her tray.
"Okay," she says quietly. "Okay, Orlando."

She doesn't ask again. She's learned not to ask me certain questions because the answers don't fit in this century, and she's too kind — or too smart — to force me to say that out loud. Instead she stands beside me against the wall, two women in bad uniforms, and we watch the room glitter without us.
The auction begins at ten.

The ballroom rearranges itself — chairs pulled to the center, the bar cordoned off, a small stage erected near the windows where the January dark presses against the glass like it wants in. The auctioneer is a tall woman with a voice like a cello bow, each syllable drawn and resonant. She brings up the lots one by one — a Cartier Tiffany, a Basquiat sketch, a pair of Ming vases the color of a winter sky — and each time the gavel falls, it lands in my chest as much as in the room. *Crack. Sold. Crack. Sold.*
Then they bring out the coat.

It's displayed under glass, lit from above with the flat, revealing light of a surgical theater, and even from twenty feet away I can see how badly they've presented it. The lighting is wrong — too harsh, flattening the wool's nap until it looks like felt instead of the heavy, napped twill it is. The positioning is worse: they've draped it on a form that's the wrong shape, too narrow in the shoulder, too long in the torso, so the coat hangs off the mannequin like it's wearing someone else's body. The lapels don't fold correctly. The belt is cinched too tight, distorting the original silhouette, and the whole effect is like looking at a photograph of someone you love, taken from a bad angle, and knowing that everyone who sees it will misunderstand the face forever.
No one in this room has earned the right to look at it.

The auctioneer tells the story. French Resistance, 1944, a woman named — and here she says a name I've never heard, a name Sotheby's invented or approximated or guessed at, a name that is not mine and never was, a name that *wraps* around the coat like a stranger's arms and claims it. She describes the sewing kit purportedly found in the lining, the "exceptional provenance," the "rare opportunity to own a piece of liberation history." The bidding starts at thirty thousand.

It climbs. Thirty-five. Forty-two. A woman in the third row raises her paddle. A man near the back. Fifty. Fifty-three. Each increment is a nail driven into something inside me, and I am very still, and I am very quiet, and I am not fine.
"Going twice at fifty-eight thousand —"
Sixty thousand. The paddle goes up near the bar, and I can't see who's holding it because my vision is doing something it shouldn't, something watery and wrong, and I blink, and it doesn't help.
"Sold. Sixty thousand dollars."
The gavel falls.
*Crack.*

My hands are shaking. Not the small tremor of too much coffee or too little sleep — this is the shaking of something structural, something load-bearing giving way. I set my tray down on the nearest surface. A guest looks at me. I don't see which one. I'm already moving, threading through the crowd, my feet carrying me toward the stage before my brain has authorized the journey. The coat is still under glass, still lit wrong, still displayed on a body that isn't mine, and I am close now, close enough to see the stitching on the lapel, close enough to see the spot where the fabric wore thin at the right elbow from me pulling my arm back to open a door — a specific door, a heavy door, a door that opened onto a staircase that went down and down and down.

I should stop. I should turn around, retrieve my tray, finish my shift, go home, lie down, breathe. I should let the coat go the way I've let everything else go — the dresses, the houses, the faces, the centuries, all of it slipping through my fingers like water through a sieve. I should be practiced at this by now.

But the coat is *right there*, under glass, under light, under the wrong name, and it looks at me the way a wound looks at a tongue — not asking, not inviting, just *being there*, open and raw and impossible to ignore.
My mouth opens.
I know I shouldn't.
I'm going to say something anyway.
